items.title: What to know before your next road trip
items.headline: What to know before your next road trip
items.description: Whether it’s a weekend escape or a long-haul adventure, a little prep goes a long way. Before you hit play on the playlist and grab the snacks, here are a few often-overlooked checks that can keep your drive fun—not frustrating. Give your car a quick health check No matter the season, a few minutes of […]
items.summary: Whether it’s a weekend escape or a long-haul adventure, a little prep goes a long way. Before you hit play on the playlist and grab the snacks, here are a few often-overlooked checks that can keep your drive fun—not frustrating. Give your car a quick health check No matter the season, a few minutes of […]
items.body: Whether it’s a weekend escape or a long-haul adventure, a little prep goes a long way. Before you hit play on the playlist and grab the snacks, here are a few often-overlooked checks that can keep your drive fun—not frustrating. Give your car a quick health check No matter the season, a few minutes of prep can prevent headaches on the road. Check tire pressure, washer fluid, lights and wipers to make sure the essentials are in good shape before you go. Conditions can change fast Weather and road conditions won’t always match what you see in the driveway. Rain, fog, or sudden detours can pop up unexpectedly. Pack a phone charger so you can quickly check conditions and navigation along the way—and travel with less stress. Proof of insurance shouldn’t require a scavenger hunt Is it in the glove box? Did you leave it in your purse you left at home? Before you leave, make sure you have proof of insurance and know exactly where to access it. Knowing where to find it makes life easier when plans change or you need it fast. The little things add up A full gas tank before long stretches of road, clear headlights, snacks within reach and a backup route just in case—these small details can make a big difference once you’re on the road. And to help you feel supported, many insurers, such as Belairdirect, offer roadside assistance if something unexpected happens. Slow down and enjoy the drive Roads can be busy with work zones, cyclists and wildlife year-round. Give yourself extra time and schedule stops to keep the trip stress-free. Some insurers reward safe driving habits with potential savings, which is another incentive to take it slow. Anytime is a good time to hit the road—keep your routine tuned up and your coverage up to date. Explore more tips and helpful car insurance information at belairdirect.com

items.title: How to prepare for severe weather risks
items.headline: How to prepare for severe weather risks
items.description: As Canadians, we have a lot of experience with wild weather swings, from bitterly cold winters to intense summer heat waves. Adapting to seasonal extremes is part of our national DNA. Canada throws a lot at us over the course of a year: melting snow, heavy rainfall, thawing and freezing cycles, strong winds, hail, severe […]
items.summary: As Canadians, we have a lot of experience with wild weather swings, from bitterly cold winters to intense summer heat waves. Adapting to seasonal extremes is part of our national DNA. Canada throws a lot at us over the course of a year: melting snow, heavy rainfall, thawing and freezing cycles, strong winds, hail, severe […]
items.body: As Canadians, we have a lot of experience with wild weather swings, from bitterly cold winters to intense summer heat waves. Adapting to seasonal extremes is part of our national DNA. Canada throws a lot at us over the course of a year: melting snow, heavy rainfall, thawing and freezing cycles, strong winds, hail, severe storms and wildfires. Taking a few practical steps now can make it easier to stay prepared and protect yourself and your property. Here are some ways you can prepare, from the home insurance experts at Co-operators: Before severe weather Have an emergency kit ready for your home and vehicle with supplies to last at least 72 hours. Create a household emergency plan so that everyone knows how to stay connected and where to go if needed. Reduce hazards around your property by clearing debris, trimming weak branches and securing outdoor items. Keep an up-to-date list of important belongings and review your insurance coverage. During severe weather Monitor weather alerts and avoid unnecessary travel. Stay clear of flooded roads and move to higher ground if you need to. During hail, stay indoors and away from windows, glass doors and skylights. During wildfire smoke events, stay indoors if possible, keep windows closed and turn off any devices such as air exchangers or heating and cooling systems that might draw in air from outside. After the weather clears Watch for hazards such as downed power lines, damaged trees or unstable structures. Look for water damage in the most common places, like basement foundation corners and along the ceilings of exterior walls. Check your sump pump to make sure it’s still working effectively. Take photos of any damage and keep receipts for cleanup, repair or lost items. Clear documentation helps your insurance company review your claim and keep the process moving during severe weather events. A bit of planning can go a long way in helping you stay safe during changing conditions. Learn more at cooperators.ca

items.title: How farmers can grow their business
items.headline: How farmers can grow their business
items.description: Farming has a long and proud history in this country. Canadian farm operators have engineered new farming practices, tools and even crops. When it comes to growing your own farm business, you know what your land, livestock or plants need. But there are some other ways to boost your business you may not have considered: […]
items.summary: Farming has a long and proud history in this country. Canadian farm operators have engineered new farming practices, tools and even crops. When it comes to growing your own farm business, you know what your land, livestock or plants need. But there are some other ways to boost your business you may not have considered: […]
items.body: Farming has a long and proud history in this country. Canadian farm operators have engineered new farming practices, tools and even crops. When it comes to growing your own farm business, you know what your land, livestock or plants need. But there are some other ways to boost your business you may not have considered: Get your brand out there with some simple marketing. A farm is a business like any other, and a strong brand can set your business apart from the competition. Developing your brand: Your farm’s brand tells your business’s story. As an example, has your farm been in the family for several generations, under the same name? Or is its identity tied closer to a feature of the land, such as a notable creek or hill? Thinking about your story can help you build a sense of your brand’s identity. Promoting your brand: Social media platforms can be great places to reach new audiences and reinforce your brand with your existing customers. You don’t have to dedicate significant time and resources to creating content. You’d be surprised how simple, authentic slice-of-life content shot around the farm is just what your audience wants. Show what you do, but also why you do it. This is a great starting point for differentiating your brand from the competition. Make data-driven decisions by getting the right information. Successful businesses often set their strategies by looking at the available data in their market. In many industries, that can mean hiring a firm to carry out market research. But one way farmers can get high-quality data at no cost is by completing the 2026 Census of Agriculture. A snapshot of Canada’s agriculture sector taken every five years, the census gives farm operators insight into current trends and challenges and lets policymakers better understand their needs and opportunities. As of May 4, farmers can fill out their census questionnaire online, and it’s important to do so—the quality of the data collected depends on wide participation. By growing a strong sense of your business’s identity and story, as well as contributing to reliable data for decision-making, you can take your farm to the next level. You can contribute to Canada’s largest source of community-level agriculture data by completing your questionnaire at census.gc.ca.

items.title: Top tips to manage arthritis-related morning stiffness
items.headline: Top tips to manage arthritis-related morning stiffness
items.description: Mornings can be the hardest part of the day when you’re living with arthritis. At rest, joints can stiffen up—either because they haven’t been moving and lubricating properly, or because inflammation builds overnight. The result is pain, stiffness and a slow start. Here are eight practical, expert-backed tips from Arthritis Society Canada to help you […]
items.summary: Mornings can be the hardest part of the day when you’re living with arthritis. At rest, joints can stiffen up—either because they haven’t been moving and lubricating properly, or because inflammation builds overnight. The result is pain, stiffness and a slow start. Here are eight practical, expert-backed tips from Arthritis Society Canada to help you […]
items.body: Mornings can be the hardest part of the day when you’re living with arthritis. At rest, joints can stiffen up—either because they haven’t been moving and lubricating properly, or because inflammation builds overnight. The result is pain, stiffness and a slow start. Here are eight practical, expert-backed tips from Arthritis Society Canada to help you loosen up and take control of your mornings. Start moving before you get up. While still in bed, gently move your joints through a comfortable range of motion for five to 10 repetitions. This can loosen the joints and reduce some of the pain and stiffness before getting up. An occupational therapist or physiotherapist can help tailor movements to your needs. Use heat to your advantage. A warm bath or shower in the morning can work wonders on stiff joints. Heating pads, electric blankets, or even a microwaved bag of rice may also be helpful. A programmable thermostat set to your preferred temperature 30 minutes before you wake up can also help make starting your day easier. Try topical relief. Warming creams, gels or oils can provide temporary relief by soothing sore joints or reducing inflammation. For hands, try applying mineral oil, wearing loose rubber gloves and soaking them in hot water while gently moving your fingers. Check your sleep setup. A supportive mattress and the right pillow can help keep your body aligned, reduce strain on your joints, and prevent awkward positions that contribute to morning stiffness. If you tend to clench your hands or bend your wrists, a splint can help keep joints in a more neutral position overnight. Upgrade your tools. Small changes at home can make a big impact. Ergonomic tools like lever-style door handles, adaptive kitchen gadgets or utensils with larger grips can reduce strain on stiff hands and make everyday tasks more manageable. Prioritize sleep. Poor sleep doesn’t just make you tired, it can also increase pain sensitivity. Creating a consistent sleep routine and improving sleep quality can help reduce both pain and stiffness in the morning. Eat right . Choose anti-inflammatory foods and avoid sugars and processed foods. Boosting your intake of foods with omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants that have been proven to reduce inflammation is another way to support your joint health. Keep moving. Too much activity can leave you sore the next day, but too little can increase stiffness. Aim for a balance of movement throughout the day, including flexibility, strength and light endurance exercises to keep joints mobile. Get more arthritis pain management tips at arthritis.ca/healthy-living

items.title: Sun Awareness Month: Sunscreen tips for kids on the go
items.headline: Sun Awareness Month: Sunscreen tips for kids on the go
items.description: Spray, play, repeat: 4 sunscreen tips for kids on the go Sun Awareness Month is a good time to refresh family sun safety habits, especially when it comes to sunscreen reapplication. For parents of active kids, the challenge is often finding fast, fuss-free ways to top up protection without interrupting the fun. Pack sunscreen like […]
items.summary: Spray, play, repeat: 4 sunscreen tips for kids on the go Sun Awareness Month is a good time to refresh family sun safety habits, especially when it comes to sunscreen reapplication. For parents of active kids, the challenge is often finding fast, fuss-free ways to top up protection without interrupting the fun. Pack sunscreen like […]
items.body: Spray, play, repeat: 4 sunscreen tips for kids on the go Sun Awareness Month is a good time to refresh family sun safety habits, especially when it comes to sunscreen reapplication. For parents of active kids, the challenge is often finding fast, fuss-free ways to top up protection without interrupting the fun. Pack sunscreen like you pack snacks. Keep it in the same pocket of your bag every time so that you’re not digging around while your child sprints toward the slide. “Kids get UV exposure during everyday outdoor activities, even when it’s cloudy, so sunscreen should be part of their daily routine,” says Dr. Katie Beleznay, a dermatologist and mom of two. “Using a broad-spectrum sunscreen, SPF 30 or higher, and reapplying at least every two hours and immediately after swimming is important to protect their skin during a day outdoors.” Set a timer. Use a phone alarm that’s loud enough for you hear outside, so that reapplication isn’t tied to memory. Set the timer for 80 minutes if your kids are likely to be sweating, and if you’re going between playgrounds, reset the timer when you arrive. Make it a game, not a stand-still order. Ask kids to freeze like a statue for five seconds, then switch to windmill arms so that you can reach elbows and wrists. One way to get even coverage on wriggly bodies is with an advanced control dual sprayer like Banana Boat Kids SmartMist Sunscreen Spray SPF 50+. The long mist covers arms and legs quickly, while the short burst targets areas like ears and neck. Pair sunscreen with shade and gear. A wide-brimmed hat, sunglasses and a quick water break under a tree can calm kids down long enough for a top-up. Consider moving play into the shade during peak afternoon sun hours. Remember to choose broad-spectrum UVA/UVB protection and use it consistently. A simple routine you can repeat every day beats an all-or-nothing approach.

items.title: Try this smoky, naturally sweet BBQ treat
items.headline: Try this smoky, naturally sweet BBQ treat
items.description: While strawberries are a staple of desserts, they can also be a secret weapon for elevating savoury main dishes. Their natural acidity and sweetness provide a sophisticated balance to salty and spicy flavours—and they add nutritional value. This barbecued chicken recipe brings bold, smoky flavour to the table, pairing juicy strawberries with chipotle chiles and […]
items.summary: While strawberries are a staple of desserts, they can also be a secret weapon for elevating savoury main dishes. Their natural acidity and sweetness provide a sophisticated balance to salty and spicy flavours—and they add nutritional value. This barbecued chicken recipe brings bold, smoky flavour to the table, pairing juicy strawberries with chipotle chiles and […]
items.body: While strawberries are a staple of desserts, they can also be a secret weapon for elevating savoury main dishes. Their natural acidity and sweetness provide a sophisticated balance to salty and spicy flavours—and they add nutritional value. This barbecued chicken recipe brings bold, smoky flavour to the table, pairing juicy strawberries with chipotle chiles and garlic for a sweet-and-spicy glaze that takes a classic grilled favourite to a new level. BBQ Chicken Drumsticks with Strawberry-Chipotle Sauce Prep time: 15 minutes Cook time: 25 minutes Servings: 4 Ingredients: 1 lb (450 g) ripe California strawberries, stemmed 3 chipotle chile peppers in adobo 2 cloves garlic 1 tbsp (15 ml) apple cider vinegar 1 tbsp (15 ml) honey 1 tsp (4 g) kosher salt, plus more for seasoning 10-12 chicken drumsticks, about 4 lbs (1,800 g) Ground black pepper for seasoning Directions: Heat a gas or charcoal grill over medium heat. Clean grill grates and brush with oil right before adding chicken. Make the sauce: Combine strawberries, chipotle peppers, garlic, vinegar, honey and salt in a blender. Blend until smooth. Taste and add more salt if needed. Pour 1 cup (250 ml) of sauce in a liquid measuring cup. Reserve the rest for serving. Prep the chicken: Meanwhile, generously season chicken with salt and pepper on all sides. Once the grill is ready, grill chicken covered for 15-20 minutes, turning every 5 minutes or so to avoid charring. After 15 minutes, or when meat starts pulling away from the bone, generously brush one side of the chicken with barbecue sauce. Cover and cook for another 5 minutes. Turn and generously brush the other side of the chicken with barbecue sauce. Cook, covered, for another 5 minutes. You should use almost all of the 1 cup of sauce. Once chicken is cooked all the way through, remove from the grill and serve with reserved barbecue sauce. By incorporating strawberries directly into the sauce, this dish naturally boosts its nutritional profile without relying on processed additives. Just one cup of strawberries provides approximately 95 per cent of your daily recommended vitamin C intake, according to Health Canada. They also contain fibre, potassium and anti-inflammatory antioxidants to help support your overall health. Their natural sweetness lets you cut back on added sugars, making this BBQ favourite a more balanced, heart-healthy option that doesn’t compromise on flavour. Find more recipes at californiastrawberries.com

items.author: Asmae Ryadi

items.title: 4 ways to support your local farmer this year
items.headline: 4 ways to support your local farmer this year
items.description: Agriculture is a vital sector that plays an important role in the lives of every Canadian. Farm operators contribute a great deal to their communities and value their community’s support. Here are some simple ways you can play a role in supporting farmers: Learn about where your food comes from. It takes an amazing amount of […]
items.summary: Agriculture is a vital sector that plays an important role in the lives of every Canadian. Farm operators contribute a great deal to their communities and value their community’s support. Here are some simple ways you can play a role in supporting farmers: Learn about where your food comes from. It takes an amazing amount of […]
items.body: Agriculture is a vital sector that plays an important role in the lives of every Canadian. Farm operators contribute a great deal to their communities and value their community’s support. Here are some simple ways you can play a role in supporting farmers: Learn about where your food comes from. It takes an amazing amount of labour, knowledge and coordination to bring farm products to market. Producers operate in networks that stretch between communities and industries. By learning more about farmers’ perspectives, challenges and operations, you can gain a greater appreciation for their work and skills. This can help you be an advocate for the agricultural community and make more informed purchasing decisions. Buy directly from farms or farmers’ markets. Stopping in at farm stands is something of a road trip tradition and has a lot to offer. Depending on the season, you can get a variety of produce, fresh-laid eggs, homemade jams and more. Farmers’ markets bring the farm stand experience closer to home. You can find a wide variety of produce and other items, such as candles, baked goods and even craft beer or cider. You can often get a better price than at the grocery store, while also feeling connected to your food and the people who produced it. Some farms also work with, or have their own, local farm basket programs, which let Canadians purchase a wide array of goods from their local farmers in a sustainable and cost-effective way. Encourage kids to explore agriculture through local programs. It’s never too early to build an appreciation for agriculture and a connection with food. There are many local programs available for children to get hands-on exposure to farms, crops and livestock. Giving kids the opportunity to explore and learn directly about the food they eat can help make it more real for them and help them grow up understanding its true value. Know where to get informed. Checking for national data from sources like the Census of Agriculture is one way to find valuable information. Collected every five years, it helps communities understand local needs by collecting data directly from farmers. This year is a census year, and farm operators across Canada will be filling out their questionnaires online. The quality of census data increases with every response, so it’s important that all farm operators participate. As of May 4, farm operators can complete their questionnaire online at census.gc.ca

items.title: When are seniors most at risk of identity theft?
items.headline: When are seniors most at risk of identity theft?
items.description: There are many reasons why fraudsters target seniors: a belief that they have accumulated savings, an assumption that they aren’t familiar with the technology scammers use or taking advantage of the fact that many seniors are socially isolated and may not have support to help them spot a scam or push back. Identity theft is […]
items.summary: There are many reasons why fraudsters target seniors: a belief that they have accumulated savings, an assumption that they aren’t familiar with the technology scammers use or taking advantage of the fact that many seniors are socially isolated and may not have support to help them spot a scam or push back. Identity theft is […]
items.body: There are many reasons why fraudsters target seniors: a belief that they have accumulated savings, an assumption that they aren’t familiar with the technology scammers use or taking advantage of the fact that many seniors are socially isolated and may not have support to help them spot a scam or push back. Identity theft is a priority for many fraudsters, since they can use that information to enrich themselves in a variety of ways. Here are a couple of the biggest threats to watch out for: Someone can pose as your bank to get your information. This is a technique called “phishing,” and it’s one of fraudsters’ favourite tactics. It works by sending a communication that seems like it’s from your bank or another institution you trust—this could be a phone call, email or text. They’ll claim there’s some issue you need to resolve quickly, like a missed payment. They may even accuse you of fraud or ask for help with a fraud investigation. The strategy is to create a sense of urgency or even panic so that you follow the included link to log in or give your information over the phone. When you do, it provides that information to the fraudster, who can use it to compromise your account to get money or even more of your identity. Tax return scams are more popular than ever. Posing as the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) is a popular tactic of fraudsters for two reasons: People take their taxes seriously. Telling someone, especially a senior, who may be on a fixed income, that there’s an issue with their tax return or benefits is an effective way to create panic. Tax information is valuable. If a fraudster can get you to give them access to your CRA account or your tax forms, they can use that information to steal your identity. To a scammer, your tax forms are a one-stop shop. How can seniors protect themselves? There are digital security services that can help, at tax time and throughout the year. Using a tool like Telus Online Security can help protect your identity. It uses built-in device security that acts as a first line of defence against phishing attacks and scams, and can help you determine which messages are legitimate and which aren’t. In the event that your identity is stolen, the service offers 24/7 access to support and identity theft specialists who can walk you through the recovery process. It’s important to be vigilant and trust your instincts. Scammers count on you not questioning what you’re told, so question it. Ask yourself if any communication you receive makes sense. If all the emails you got from them in the past told you to log into your account directly, why does this one have a link to click? The threat of identity theft has never been higher, but by protecting yourself and exercising caution and judgment, you can keep yourself safer. Learn how you can better protect your identity and finances during tax season and beyond at telus.com/onlinesecurity

items.title: 5 insurance tools you may not know you have
items.headline: 5 insurance tools you may not know you have
items.description: Between work, family and everyday responsibilities, most people don’t have time to explore everything their insurance offers. Once your policy is set up, it’s easy to forget about it until you need it. But it’s worth taking a closer look because there are more tools available at your fingertips than you might realize. Some insurance […]
items.summary: Between work, family and everyday responsibilities, most people don’t have time to explore everything their insurance offers. Once your policy is set up, it’s easy to forget about it until you need it. But it’s worth taking a closer look because there are more tools available at your fingertips than you might realize. Some insurance […]
items.body: Between work, family and everyday responsibilities, most people don’t have time to explore everything their insurance offers. Once your policy is set up, it’s easy to forget about it until you need it. But it’s worth taking a closer look because there are more tools available at your fingertips than you might realize. Some insurance companies, like Belairdirect, offer digital tools that can make your insurance experience simpler. They help save time, reduce stress and make managing insurance easier. Here are five useful things you may not know you can do today: Manage your policies anytime No more waiting for office hours or making phone calls. With digital insurance tools, you can view policy documents, billing information and claims details through secure online portals or mobile apps. Many changes, such as updating your address, coverage options, drivers or vehicles, can be done right from your computer or smartphone. Access proof of car insurance instantly We’ve all been there: searching the glove box, digging through paperwork at home or realizing your proof of insurance is buried in your unopened mail. Digital tools let you access proof of car insurance directly from your phone or app, so it’s always there when you need it. Get helpful tips, alerts and insights Many insurers offer seasonal reminders, weather alerts and actionable tips to protect your home and vehicle. Some also provide driving insights and maintenance tips designed to support safer habits and keep your car in good condition year-round. Submit and track claims online Submitting a claim online or through an app often means a faster response time and fewer steps. You can upload photos, track the status of your claim and receive updates in real time, making the process smoother and less stressful. Make payments quickly and securely Busy schedules make it easy to forget due dates. Digital payment options allow you to view your balance, make payments securely and stay up to date. If you haven’t explored what’s available lately, now’s a great time. By going digital, you can manage your coverage with confidence whether you’re at home, at work or on the go. You can learn more at belairdirect.com

items.title: Planning for retirement: Protect what matters in your next chapter
items.headline: Planning for retirement: Protect what matters in your next chapter
items.description: Retirement is one of life’s most exciting milestones. After years of hard work, it’s time to focus on what’s next, whether that means slowing down, trying something new or finally doing more of what you love. With these lifestyle changes comes an important opportunity: reviewing your insurance coverage to ensure it reflects how you live […]
items.summary: Retirement is one of life’s most exciting milestones. After years of hard work, it’s time to focus on what’s next, whether that means slowing down, trying something new or finally doing more of what you love. With these lifestyle changes comes an important opportunity: reviewing your insurance coverage to ensure it reflects how you live […]
items.body: Retirement is one of life’s most exciting milestones. After years of hard work, it’s time to focus on what’s next, whether that means slowing down, trying something new or finally doing more of what you love. With these lifestyle changes comes an important opportunity: reviewing your insurance coverage to ensure it reflects how you live today, not how you lived years ago. Driving less Many retirees drive less once they step away from daily commuting. Fewer kilometres, fewer vehicles or a different type of car can all change what coverage makes sense. Some insurance companies, such as Belairdirect, can help review your policy to see if driving less or owning just one vehicle could lower your costs or reduce coverage you no longer need. Downsizing or relocating As life changes, so do housing needs. Perhaps the kids have moved out and you’re ready for a smaller, lower-maintenance condo that gives you more freedom to travel or enjoy hobbies. Downsizing can also mean simplifying: less space to maintain, fewer belongings and often less to insure. If you’re moving from a single-family home to a condo or apartment, your coverage needs can look very different. Asking your insurer a few key questions can help clarify the right level of coverage and prevent unnecessary costs. Spending more time travelling If your retirement plans include seeing more of the world, then you may not need your car for a while. Ask your insurance provider whether you can suspend coverage while your vehicle is in storage. Vehicle storage insurance can help protect both your car and your finances. Spending more time at home For some, retirement means staying put and investing more time and care into the home they love. Renovations, upgrades and regular maintenance not only improve comfort but can also help protect and increase your home’s value over time. Keeping your insurance up to date as you make changes ensures those improvements are fully covered. After all, your home isn’t just a building, it’s where memories were made and where new ones will continue to grow. Retirement is about enjoying the freedom you’ve earned. Taking the time to review your coverage now can help ensure you’re protected, so you can focus on making the most of this exciting next chapter. You can find more useful tips at belairdirect.com

After years of hard work, it’s time to focus on what’s next, whether that means slowing down, trying something new or finally doing more of what you love. With these lifestyle changes comes an important opportunity: reviewing your insurance coverage to ensure it reflects how you live today, not how you lived years ago. Driving less Many retirees drive less once they step away from daily commuting. Fewer kilometres, fewer vehicles or a different type of car can all change what coverage makes sense. Some insurance companies, such as Belairdirect, can help review your policy to see if driving less or owning just one vehicle could lower your costs or reduce coverage you no longer need. Downsizing or relocating As life changes, so do housing needs. Perhaps the kids have moved out and you’re ready for a smaller, lower-maintenance condo that gives you more freedom to travel or enjoy hobbies. Downsizing can also mean simplifying: less space to maintain, fewer belongings and often less to insure. If you’re moving from a single-family home to a condo or apartment, your coverage needs can look very different. Asking your insurer a few key questions can help clarify the right level of coverage and prevent unnecessary costs. Spending more time travelling If your retirement plans include seeing more of the world, then you may not need your car for a while. Ask your insurance provider whether you can suspend coverage while your vehicle is in storage. Vehicle storage insurance can help protect both your car and your finances. Spending more time at home For some, retirement means staying put and investing more time and care into the home they love. Renovations, upgrades and regular maintenance not only improve comfort but can also help protect and increase your home’s value over time. Keeping your insurance up to date as you make changes ensures those improvements are fully covered. After all, your home isn’t just a building, it’s where memories were made and where new ones will continue to grow. Retirement is about enjoying the freedom you’ve earned. Taking the time to review your coverage now can help ensure you’re protected, so you can focus on making the most of this exciting next chapter. You can find more useful tips at belairdirect.com
